trajectory_summary: The call moves efficiently from introduction to booked appointment in under five minutes, but the booking is built on an unresolved product-fit question. Larry wants solar for a newly built, no-meter detached garage — a non-standard use case the rep never confirmed Ion can serve. The rep held scope correctly on pricing but declared qualification prematurely, and the appointment is fragile because the closer will arrive to a customer who doesn't know the company name and whose core ask may not match Ion's offering.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown, but the observable outcome is a booked in-person visit. That outcome should be flagged as conditionally fragile: the appointment was booked without confirming Ion's ability to solar a no-meter detached garage, without a bill or usage anchor, and with a customer who misidentified the company name. Energy built on the surface; structural durability is low.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Premature qualification declaration on a non-standard use case
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, yeah, Larry, then we got you all qualified. I have everything I need here on my side.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep booked an in-person visit for a no-meter detached garage solar install without confirming Ion can serve that use case, without a credit check, and without a bill anchor. 'Qualified' was declared on address + utility + dimensions alone. On a standard residential call this would be a verify-completion gap; on a non-standard use case it's a product-fit risk that could send a specialist to a site that doesn't qualify. The manager needs to establish with the rep what 'qualified' means as a checklist, and separately confirm with Kenny whether Ion serves no-meter detached structures — because the rep needs that product knowledge before the next similar call.

key_moments.quote: Okay. So and does that garage have its own meter?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep correctly identifies that meter configuration is a product-fit diagnostic — a no-meter garage changes the install design entirely. This is a smart instinct. The failure is that the rep gets the answer ('No. It doesn't.') and moves on without flagging whether Ion can actually serve a no-meter structure. The question was asked; the implication was never resolved.

key_moments.quote: And then what utility company are you with, Larry?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep asks utility company — a verify item — but never follows with the average bill question. The natural sequencing in the codex is utility → bill, because the utility question opens the door to 'and what are you paying on average?' Larry answered BGE and the rep moved directly to prior designs. The bill anchor — which the codex calls 'THE anchor for the entire pitch' — was never established. For a garage-only solar install the bill anchor is admittedly non-standard, but the rep didn't even attempt to understand Larry's energy cost motivation.

Larry misremembers the company name — he's booking an appointment with a company he can't correctly identify. The rep responded 'Correct.' at 03:09, which ambiguously validates the wrong name rather than gently correcting it. A customer who doesn't know who they're meeting with is a soft no-show risk: when the text arrives from 'ION Solar' it may not match his mental model of the appointment. The codex's intro section emphasizes legitimacy establishment in the first 30 seconds — this moment reveals that legitimacy was not fully anchored.

key_moments.quote: No. I haven't spoke with anyone else yet, but I am gonna call for other references.
key_moments.stewart_read: Larry signals he's shopping — he hasn't seen other designs yet but intends to. The rep responded 'Okay. Awesome. Yeah. No. Good. Okay. Sweet.' and moved immediately to closing the appointment. Strategic read: the rep correctly prioritized locking the appointment before Larry shops, which is the right instinct — a booked appointment before competitors get in is a real advantage. Reactive read: the rep missed an opportunity to create urgency or differentiation that would make Larry less likely to cancel after talking to competitors. The strategic read is stronger here — booking first is the right move — but the rep left the differentiation moment on the table entirely.
